content.text: A prompt version registry is a structured store that tracks every version of every system prompt in your application, records which version is currently deployed, and provides atomic rollback to any previous version. Without one, prompt changes are ad-hoc text edits that are hard to trace, compare, or reverse quickly under production pressure.
Why Prompts Need a Registry

Prompts share the same lifecycle risks as application code: a change can introduce a regression, a rollback must be possible within minutes, and the history of what changed and when must be auditable. Yet prompts often live in environment variables, configuration files, or — worst — hardcoded strings scattered through the codebase. A registry centralises this:
Immutability.

Once a version is registered, its content never changes. If you need to make a correction, you register a new version. This guarantees that any eval run tagged with a version ID can be reproduced exactly.
Single source of truth.
The registry holds the current pointer. All application instances read the current prompt from the registry, not from their local config. A deployment is a pointer change, not a code deploy.
Audit log.
Every version records who authored it, when it was registered, and what eval score it achieved before deployment. This log is the forensic record when a regression is discovered days after the fact.
Registry Data Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
import hashlib, time

@dataclass
class PromptVersion:
    version_id: str              # e.g. "v4" or a semantic version "2.3.1"
    content_hash: str            # SHA-256 of the system prompt text
    system_prompt: str
    model: str                   # model this prompt was authored for
    author: str
    created_at: float
    eval_score: float | None     # score before promotion to current
    eval_suite_hash: str | None  # which suite was used for the eval
    status: str                  # draft | canary | deployed | retired
    notes: str = ""

def make_version(
    version_id: str,
    system_prompt: str,
    model: str,
    author: str,
    notes: str = "",
) -> PromptVersion:
    content_hash = hashlib.sha256(system_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]
    return PromptVersion(
        version_id=version_id,
        content_hash=content_hash,
        system_prompt=system_prompt,
        model=model,
        author=author,
        created_at=time.time(),
        eval_score=None,
        eval_suite_hash=None,
        status="draft",
        notes=notes,
    )
Implementing the Registry
A SQLite-backed registry is sufficient for most teams. For multi-region deployments where prompt reads must be fast, use a key-value store (Redis, DynamoDB) with the same logical model:

import sqlite3, json, time

class PromptRegistry:
    def __init__(self, db_path: str = "prompt_registry.db"):
        self.conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path, check_same_thread=False)
        self._init_schema()

    def _init_schema(self) -> None:
        self.conn.executescript("""
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS versions (
                version_id    TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
                content_hash  TEXT NOT NULL,
                system_prompt TEXT NOT NULL,
                model         TEXT NOT NULL,
                author        TEXT NOT NULL,
                created_at    REAL NOT NULL,
                eval_score    REAL,
                suite_hash    TEXT,
                status        TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
                notes         TEXT
            );
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS current_pointer (
                id            INTEGER PRIMARY KEY CHECK (id = 1),
                version_id    TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES versions(version_id),
                updated_at    REAL NOT NULL,
                updated_by    TEXT NOT NULL
            );
        """)
        self.conn.commit()

    def register(self, version: "PromptVersion") -> None:
        self.conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO versions
               (version_id, content_hash, system_prompt, model, author,
                created_at, eval_score, suite_hash, status, notes)
               VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)""",
            (version.version_id, version.content_hash, version.system_prompt,
             version.model, version.author, version.created_at,
             version.eval_score, version.eval_suite_hash, version.status, version.notes),
        )
        self.conn.commit()

    def set_eval_result(self, version_id: str, score: float, suite_hash: str) -> None:
        self.conn.execute(
            "UPDATE versions SET eval_score=?, suite_hash=? WHERE version_id=?",
            (score, suite_hash, version_id),
        )
        self.conn.commit()

    def deploy(self, version_id: str, deployed_by: str) -> None:
        # Mark old current as retired
        old = self.get_current_version_id()
        if old:
            self.conn.execute(
                "UPDATE versions SET status='retired' WHERE version_id=?", (old,)
            )
        # Set new current
        self.conn.execute(
            """INSERT INTO current_pointer (id, version_id, updated_at, updated_by)
               VALUES (1, ?, ?, ?)
               ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET
                   version_id=excluded.version_id,
                   updated_at=excluded.updated_at,
                   updated_by=excluded.updated_by""",
            (version_id, time.time(), deployed_by),
        )
        self.conn.execute(
            "UPDATE versions SET status='deployed' WHERE version_id=?", (version_id,)
        )
        self.conn.commit()

    def rollback(self, target_version_id: str, rolled_back_by: str) -> None:
        self.deploy(target_version_id, rolled_back_by)

    def get_current(self) -> str | None:
        row = self.conn.execute(
            "SELECT v.system_prompt FROM current_pointer cp JOIN versions v ON cp.version_id=v.version_id"
        ).fetchone()
        return row[0] if row else None

    def get_current_version_id(self) -> str | None:
        row = self.conn.execute("SELECT version_id FROM current_pointer WHERE id=1").fetchone()
        return row[0] if row else None

    def history(self) -> list[dict]:
        rows = self.conn.execute(
            """SELECT version_id, content_hash, model, author, created_at,
                      eval_score, status, notes
               FROM versions ORDER BY created_at DESC"""
        ).fetchall()
        return [dict(zip(["version_id","content_hash","model","author","created_at",
                          "eval_score","status","notes"], r)) for r in rows]
Integrating the Registry into the Deploy Pipeline
The registry gates promotion through three phases: author, eval, deploy. No version can move from draft to deployed without passing the eval gate:

async def safe_promote(
    registry: PromptRegistry,
    version_id: str,
    suite_path: str,
    threshold: float,
    deployer: str,
) -> dict:
    """Run eval gate and promote only if score exceeds threshold."""
    # 1. Run eval
    result = await run_eval(suite_path, model="claude-opus-4-8")
    registry.set_eval_result(version_id, result["score"], result["suite_hash"])

    if result["score"] < threshold:
        return {
            "promoted": False,
            "reason": f"Eval score {result['score']:.3f} below threshold {threshold}",
            "version_id": version_id,
        }

    # 2. Promote
    registry.deploy(version_id, deployer)
    return {
        "promoted": True,
        "version_id": version_id,
        "score": result["score"],
    }
Rollback Procedures
Three rollback scenarios require different response times:
Emergency Rollback — Under 2 Minutes
When an error rate spike or safety violation is detected, the on-call engineer needs to restore the last known-good version within minutes. The registry makes this a single call:

def emergency_rollback(registry: PromptRegistry, operator: str) -> str:
    """Rollback to the most recent retired version with a passing eval score."""
    history = registry.history()
    current_id = registry.get_current_version_id()

    # Find most recent previously-deployed version that had a passing score
    candidates = [
        v for v in history
        if v["status"] == "retired"
        and v["eval_score"] is not None
        and v["version_id"] != current_id
    ]
    if not candidates:
        raise ValueError("No previous version available for rollback")

    target = candidates[0]   # history() returns newest-first
    registry.rollback(target["version_id"], operator)
    return target["version_id"]

# In a runbook or PagerDuty automation:
# restored_to = emergency_rollback(registry, "on-call-bot")
# print(f"Rolled back to {restored_to}")
Planned Rollback — Canary Failure
When a canary fails its gate, rollback means setting the canary percentage back to 0. The new version stays in the registry with status 'draft' for investigation — its content is not deleted:

def rollback_canary(router, registry: PromptRegistry, failed_version_id: str) -> None:
    """Return all traffic to the control; leave failed version in registry for post-mortem."""
    router.set_canary_percentage(0)
    registry.conn.execute(
        "UPDATE versions SET status='failed_canary' WHERE version_id=?",
        (failed_version_id,)
    )
    registry.conn.commit()
    print(f"Canary {failed_version_id} rolled back. Traffic restored to control.")
Diff and Audit Tools
When a regression is discovered, the first question is what changed. The registry enables fast diffs between any two versions:

import difflib

def diff_versions(registry: PromptRegistry, v_a: str, v_b: str) -> str:
    rows = dict(registry.conn.execute(
        "SELECT version_id, system_prompt FROM versions WHERE version_id IN (?,?)", (v_a, v_b)
    ).fetchall())
    if v_a not in rows or v_b not in rows:
        raise ValueError(f"One or both versions not found: {v_a}, {v_b}")
    a_lines = rows[v_a].splitlines(keepends=True)
    b_lines = rows[v_b].splitlines(keepends=True)
    return "".join(difflib.unified_diff(a_lines, b_lines, fromfile=v_a, tofile=v_b))

def audit_log(registry: PromptRegistry) -> None:
    rows = registry.conn.execute(
        """SELECT v.version_id, v.author, v.created_at, v.eval_score, v.status,
                  cp.updated_by, cp.updated_at
           FROM versions v
           LEFT JOIN current_pointer cp ON cp.version_id = v.version_id
           ORDER BY v.created_at DESC"""
    ).fetchall()
    print(f"{'Version':<12} {'Author':<15} {'Score':>6}  {'Status':<16}  {'Deployed By':<15}")
    print("-" * 70)
    for row in rows:
        vid, author, created, score, status, deployed_by, _ = row
        score_str = f"{score:.3f}" if score is not None else "  N/A"
        print(f"{vid:<12} {author:<15} {score_str:>6}  {status:<16}  {(deployed_by or ''):<15}")
Storing Prompts in Git vs a Registry
Approach
Pros
Cons
When to Use
Git only
Free, familiar, diffs built-in
No runtime lookup; deploy = code deploy
Simple apps, prompts change rarely
Registry only
Runtime changeable, atomic rollback
Another service to operate
Frequent prompt iteration, fast rollback needed
Git + Registry
Git as source of truth; registry as runtime store
Sync discipline required
Teams that want both auditability and runtime control

The git + registry pattern is the most common in production: prompt source files live in a git repo (with PR reviews and blame history), and a CI step registers each merged version into the runtime registry. The registry serves the current pointer; git holds the canonical history.

# .github/workflows/prompt-deploy.yml
# Runs when a prompt file changes on main
- name: Register new prompt version
  run: |
    python scripts/register_prompt.py \
      --file prompts/customer_support.txt \
      --version ${{ github.sha }} \
      --author ${{ github.actor }} \
      --registry-url ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_URL }}
Summary
A prompt version registry makes prompt changes as controlled as code changes: versioned, gated by eval, deployable atomically, and rollback-able in minutes.
Register every prompt version with a content hash, author, and eval score before deployment
The registry current pointer is the single source of truth — all application instances read from it
Versions are immutable; rollback is a pointer change, not a content edit
Emergency rollback restores the last known-good version in under two minutes via a single registry call
Use git as the source of truth for prompt history; the registry as the runtime deployment target
Pair the registry with the eval gate: no version is deployed without a passing offline eval score
